trajectory_summary: Carter opens with one clean schema execution — the time-constraint reframe — that earns him the five minutes Shirley was about to deny him. From there the call is functional but thin: verify is partial, the credit-DQ moment is mishandled as a gate rather than a bridge, and the button-up is stripped to bare bones. The booking is real but fragile — the closer inherits an unresolved credit-score question, no bill data, no roof data, and a customer whose last stated concern was payment structure.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked Monday 11AM appointment, so a 'booked' outcome tag would be accurate. However, the booking is structurally incomplete — no button-up protocol elements were executed (one-hour block, computer/no-driving ask, 'is that fair' handshake, text follow-up promise). The sit is at elevated no-show risk.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Credit qualifier delivery — gate vs. bridge
primary_coaching_focus.quote: the main requirement to get solar is having a credit score above 6 50. Is that the case for you?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Carter delivers the credit qualifier as a hard gate with no alternative path, then has nothing prepared when Shirley says no. The call recovers only because Shirley self-advocates. On a less engaged customer, this is where the call dies. The fix is structural: build the alternative path ('we also have purchase options') into the qualifier delivery so a 'no' answer opens a door rather than closing one. The threshold error (650 vs. 670) is a secondary correction the manager should address in the same conversation.

key_moments.quote: All I'm doing is trying to schedule something. So if you have a quick five minutes right now, I'll just run through it super quick, let you get back to your family, and then we can set something up for next week.
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter executes the time-constraint protocol correctly: he names the time ask explicitly, frames the benefit to Shirley, and anchors the outcome. This is the call's one proactive schema-aligned move and it directly earns the five minutes Shirley was about to deny. Strategic read holds — this is not luck, it's the right move executed cleanly.

Two months in the home is the root cause of every subsequent knowledge gap — roof age unknown, utility company unknown, bill history nonexistent. Carter captures the residence length but does not register its downstream implication: this customer cannot produce a utility bill on this call, and the closer will arrive without usage data. The verify item is checked; the implication is missed.

key_moments.quote: No. Do I know how old it is? No. But Yeah. It's not that old. It's in my package. I'd have to go and look it up.
key_moments.stewart_read: Roof age is a hard-DQ trigger if over 15 years with no replacement planned (dq_rules.roof_too_old_no_replacement). Carter accepts 'I'd have to look it up' and moves on without flagging the gap or noting it for the closer. The closer cannot complete qualification without this data — and if the roof turns out to be over 15 years, the cross-sell to roofing (cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned) becomes the only revenue path.

key_moments.quote: Oh, I don't know. I and, you know, everything's so new to me. Name something. And then, is it Las Vegas Power? I don't know. I'd have to go look that one up too.
key_moments.stewart_read: Shirley's 'everything's so new to me' is not just a knowledge gap — it's a compounding new-homeowner signal. She has no bill history, no utility familiarity, and no roof data. Carter resolves the utility question by looking it up himself (a reasonable workaround) but does not register that bill collection is structurally impossible on this call. The enthusiasm signal buried here: a brand-new homeowner who filled out a solar form within two months of moving in is a high-intent lead — she's thinking about solar before she's even unpacked. Carter walks past this entirely.
